title: शिवाष्टकम् (तस्मै नमः)
devanagariText:
॥ श्री शिवाष्टकम् ॥

तस्मै नमः परमकारणकारणाय
दीप्तोज्ज्वलज्ज्वलितपिङ्गललोचनाय।
नागेन्द्रहाराय त्रिलोचनाय
भस्माङ्गरागाय महेश्वराय॥ १॥

तस्मै नमः परमकारणकारणाय
दक्षप्रजापतिमखच्छिदे ताण्डवाय।
वृषाङ्कमौलिकृतचन्द्रगिरीश्वराय
व्यालप्रियालय महेश्वराय॥ २॥

तस्मै नमः परमकारणकारणाय
सृष्टिस्थितिप्रलयकालमहेश्वराय।
विश्वेश्वराय सकलेश्वरशूलपाणये
शेषाय शुद्धतमरूपमहेश्वराय॥ ३॥

तस्मै नमः परमकारणकारणाय
कैलासवासविलसच्छिवलिङ्गमौलये।
गौरीनिरन्तरविभूषितवामभागं
नारायणप्रियमनङ्गमदापहाय॥ ४॥

तस्मै नमः परमकारणकारणाय
पञ्चाननाय फणिराजविभूषणाय।
उमापतये भवनाशनकारणाय
सिद्धान्तमौलिपरितोषितमानसाय॥ ५॥

तस्मै नमः परमकारणकारणाय
कर्पूरकुन्दधवलेन्दुजटाधराय।
गङ्गाधराय गजलक्ष्मणसंहराय
दक्षाध्वराय भगनेत्रविभेदनाय॥ ६॥

तस्मै नमः परमकारणकारणाय
अव्यक्तरूपनिगमागमवेद्यमूर्तये।
वाचामतीतमनसश्चिन्त्यतत्त्वरूपं
चिद्रूपमव्ययमहामृतस्वरूपाय॥ ७॥

तस्मै नमः परमकारणकारणाय
नन्दीश्वराय वृषभध्वजसंयुताय।
त्र्यम्बकाय हरिणाङ्किततानपात्रे
वेदान्तवेद्यमहसे शिवतत्त्वरूपाय॥ ८॥

॥ इति श्री शिवाष्टकम् सम्पूर्णम् ॥
transliteration:
|| Shri Shivashtakam ||

Tasmai Namah Paramakaaranakaaranaya
Diptojjvalajjvalitapingalalochanaaya |
Nagendraharaya Trilochanaaya
Bhasmangaraagaya Maheshvaraya || 1 ||

Tasmai Namah Paramakaaranakaaranaya
Daksha-prajapati-makha-chchhide Tandavaya |
Vrishankamauli-krita-chandra-girishvaraya
Vyaala-priyalaya Maheshvaraya || 2 ||

Tasmai Namah Paramakaaranakaaranaya
Srishti-sthiti-pralaya-kala-maheshvaraya |
Vishveshvaraya Sakaleshvara-shoolapanaye
Sheshaya Shuddhatama-roopa-maheshvaraya || 3 ||

Tasmai Namah Paramakaaranakaaranaya
Kailasa-vasa-vilasach-chiva-linga-maulaye |
Gauri-nirantara-vibhooshita-vama-bhagam
Narayana-priyam-ananga-madapahaya || 4 ||

Tasmai Namah Paramakaaranakaaranaya
Panchananaya Phani-raja-vibhooshanaya |
Uma-pataye Bhava-nashana-kaaranaya
Siddhanta-mauli-paritoshita-manasaya || 5 ||

Tasmai Namah Paramakaaranakaaranaya
Karpoora-kunda-dhavala-indu-jatadharaya |
Gangadharaya Gaja-lakshman-samharaya
Dakshadhvaraya Bhaga-netra-vibhedanaya || 6 ||

Tasmai Namah Paramakaaranakaaranaya
Avyakta-roopa-nigamagama-vedya-moortaye |
Vacham-atita-manasash-chintya-tattva-roopam
Chid-roopam-avyaya-maha-amrita-svaroopaya || 7 ||

Tasmai Namah Paramakaaranakaaranaya
Nandishvaraya Vrishabha-dhvaja-samyutaya |
Tryambakaya Harinankita-tana-patre
Vedanta-vedya-mahase Shiva-tattva-roopaya || 8 ||

|| Iti Shri Shivashtakam Sampurnam ||
hindiMeaning:
उन परमेश्वर को नमस्कार जो परम कारण के भी कारण हैं, जिनके नेत्र प्रज्ज्वलित पिंगल वर्ण के हैं, जो नागेन्द्र का हार धारण करते हैं, जो त्रिलोचन हैं, जिनके अंग भस्म से विभूषित हैं — उन महेश्वर को नमन॥ १॥

उन्हें नमस्कार जिन्होंने दक्ष प्रजापति के यज्ञ को विध्वंस किया, जो तांडव नृत्य करते हैं, जिनके मस्तक पर चन्द्रमा शोभित है — उन वृषभध्वज महेश्वर को नमन॥ २॥

उन्हें नमस्कार जो सृष्टि, स्थिति और प्रलय काल के स्वामी हैं, जो विश्वेश्वर हैं, शूलपाणि हैं, शुद्धतम रूप वाले महेश्वर हैं॥ ३॥

कैलास में विराजमान, शिवलिंग मौलि, गौरी से वामभाग विभूषित, नारायण के प्रिय, कामदेव के मद का नाश करने वाले — उन्हें नमन॥ ४॥
benefits: Invokes the supreme blessings of lord shiva
Classical tradition invokes this for removal of sins and worldly attachments
Invokes the blessing of spiritual wisdom and inner peace
Traditionally invoked for protection from all calamities and evil forces
Ideal for Maha Shivaratri and Monday worship
description: Among the most cherished prayers to Lord Shiva, Shiva Ashtakam (Tasmai Namah) (शिवाष्टकम् (तस्मै नमः)) stands as an eight-verse devotional hymn. Drawn from Traditional Shaivite Literature, this text has been cherished by devotees for centuries. Chanting this stotra with devotion is said to remove all sins and worldly attachments as well as bestow spiritual wisdom and inner peace. Additionally, it is known to grant the supreme blessings of Lord Shiva. It is especially recommended on Monday (Somvar) and during Maha Shivaratri and Shravan Somvar for maximum spiritual benefit. With 8 verses and a reading time of about 8 minutes, this prayer is ideal for both daily worship and special occasions. On this page, you can read the complete Shiva Ashtakam (Tasmai Namah) in Devanagari Sanskrit with English transliteration, Hindi meaning (arth), and free PDF download for offline recitation.
seoDescription: Read Shiva Ashtakam (Tasmai Namah) in Sanskrit with transliteration and Hindi meaning. 8-verse hymn glorifying Lord Shiva as the supreme cause.
alsoKnownAs: Shivashtakam Tasmai Namah Eight Verse Hymn
Shiva Ashtakam Cause of All Causes

seoFaqs.question: What is the Shiva Ashtakam (Tasmai Namah)?
seoFaqs.answer: Shiva Ashtakam (शिवाष्टकम्) — also called the "Tasmai Namah" Shivashtakam — is an eight-verse Sanskrit hymn dedicated to Lord Shiva. Each of the eight verses begins with the powerful refrain "Tasmai Namah Parama-Karana-Karanaya" — "Salutations to Him, the Cause of all causes" — establishing Shiva as the ultimate metaphysical first cause (parama-karana) beyond which there is no further cause. The eight verses progressively glorify Shiva as: (1) the wearer of serpent garlands and three-eyed Maheshwara; (2) the destroyer of Daksha's sacrifice and performer of the Tandava; (3) the Lord of creation, sustenance and dissolution (srishti-sthiti-pralaya); (4) the resident of Kailash, beloved of Narayana, slayer of Kamadeva's pride; (5) the five-faced (panchanana) Lord of Uma; (6) the Ganga-bearer with camphor-white matted locks; (7) the formless one knowable through the Vedas; and (8) Nandishvara, Tryambaka, the supreme Shiva-tattva. (Source: Traditional Shaivite literature.)

seoFaqs.question: What does "Tasmai Namah Parama Karana Karanaya" mean?
seoFaqs.answer: "Tasmai Namah Parama-Karana-Karanaya" (तस्मै नमः परमकारणकारणाय) is the powerful refrain that opens every verse of the Shiva Ashtakam, and it translates as: "Salutations (namah) to Him (tasmai) who is the cause (karana) of the supreme cause (parama-karana)." In Hindu metaphysics, the "karana" (cause) is the source from which an effect arises. The world has its own karana, and even that karana has a still higher karana — but Shiva is the parama-karana-karana: the ultimate cause beyond all causes, the unmoved mover, the source of even the highest principle. This refrain establishes Shiva's position in Shaiva Siddhanta as the supreme parameshwara (highest Lord) — beyond Brahma, Vishnu, the gunas, the elements, the cosmic mind. The repetition across eight verses drives this central truth into the devotee's awareness. (Source: Shiva Ashtakam refrain; Shaiva Siddhanta metaphysics.)

seoFaqs.question: What are the iconographic descriptions of Shiva in this Ashtakam?
seoFaqs.answer: The Shiva Ashtakam paints the complete iconography of Lord Shiva across its eight verses: (1) verse 1 — Nagendrahara (king of serpents as garland), Trilochana (three-eyed), Bhasmangaraga (body smeared with sacred ash), Diptojjvala-Pingala-Lochana (radiant tawny eyes); (2) verse 2 — Daksha-Yajna-Vidhvamsaka (destroyer of Daksha's sacrifice), Tandavakara (performer of cosmic dance), Vrishanka-Mauli (crescent-on-head); (3) verse 3 — Vishveshvara (Lord of universe), Shulapani (trident-bearer); (4) verse 4 — Kailasa-vasi (Kailash-dweller), Gauri-vibhushita-vama-bhaga (Gauri on left side), Narayana-priya; (5) verse 5 — Panchanana (five-faced), Phaniraja-vibhushana (king-cobra ornaments), Umapati; (6) verse 6 — Karpura-Kunda-Dhavala-Indu-Jatadhara (camphor-white moon in matted hair), Gangadhara; (7) verse 7 — Avyakta-Rupa (formless), Chid-Rupa (consciousness-form); (8) verse 8 — Nandishvara, Vrishabhadhvaja (bull-bannered), Tryambaka (three-eyed). (Source: Shiva Ashtakam verses 1-8.)

seoFaqs.question: What is the story of Daksha-yajna referenced in verse 2?
seoFaqs.answer: Verse 2 of the Shiva Ashtakam refers to the famous Puranic narrative of the destruction of Daksha Prajapati's sacrifice (Daksha-yajna-vidhvamsa). According to the Shiva Mahapurana and Bhagavata Purana, Daksha — Sati's father — performed a great sacrifice without inviting Shiva and Sati out of contempt for Shiva's ash-smeared, ascetic appearance. Sati, unable to bear the insult to her husband, immolated herself in the sacrificial fire (this is the origin of the word "sati"). Enraged, Shiva tore a lock of his matted hair, from which arose the fierce Virabhadra, who destroyed Daksha's sacrifice and beheaded Daksha himself. Later, on the prayers of the gods, Shiva relented and revived Daksha with a goat's head. This narrative establishes Shiva's righteous destructive aspect (samhara-kartri) — the destroyer of pride, ego, and incomplete dharma. The Tandava-nritya (cosmic dance) referenced in the same verse is connected to this episode. (Source: Shiva Mahapurana; Bhagavata Purana 4th canto.)

seoFaqs.question: What are the benefits of reciting Shiva Ashtakam?
seoFaqs.answer: Shiva Ashtakam is preserved in classical Shaivite tradition as a powerful eight-verse stotra granting Shiva's supreme blessings. Specific benefits include: (1) cultivation of clear awareness of Shiva as parama-karana — the ultimate cause — strengthening Vedanta-vichara; (2) removal of all sins (sarva-papa-nashaka) and worldly attachments (sansara-bandha-mochana); (3) bestowal of spiritual wisdom (jnana) and inner peace (manasika shanti); (4) protection from all calamities and evil forces (sarva-bhaya-nashaka, sarva-vighna-nashaka); (5) destruction of Kamadeva's pride — i.e., the conquest of lust and worldly desire (verse 4); (6) deep iconographic visualisation (dhyana) of Shiva's complete form across eight verses; (7) fulfilment of the supreme blessing of Lord Shiva and the ultimate goal of moksha. Recommended on Mondays, Maha Shivaratri, and Shravan Somvar. (Source: classical Shaivite stotra tradition.)

seoFaqs.question: How is Shiva Ashtakam (Tasmai Namah) different from other Shiva Ashtakams?
seoFaqs.answer: Several stotras bear the name "Shiva Ashtakam" (literally "hymn of eight verses to Shiva"). The Tasmai Namah Shivashtakam is distinguished from the others by: (1) its unique opening refrain "Tasmai Namah Parama-Karana-Karanaya" which begins every single verse — a highly distinctive structural feature; (2) its philosophical emphasis on Shiva as the metaphysical first cause (parama-karana), rooted in Shaiva Siddhanta theology; (3) its inclusion of the Daksha-yajna narrative (verse 2) and the Kamadeva-conquest (verse 4) — major Puranic episodes; (4) its concluding declaration of Shiva as Shiva-tattva-rupa (the form of the Shiva-principle itself, verse 8). Other famous Shivashtakams include: "Prabhum Pranatha-Pala" Shivashtakam, "Namami Shankaram" Shivashtakam, the Shivashtakam from the Padma Purana, and the Shivashtakam from the Brahma Purana. Each has its own meter, focus, and tradition of recitation. (Source: classical Shaivite stotra literature.)

seoFaqs.question: When should Shiva Ashtakam (Tasmai Namah) be chanted?
seoFaqs.answer: Shiva Ashtakam is preserved in classical Shaivite tradition as a daily Shiva stotra suitable at any time, with the strongest benefit obtained at the following times: (1) all Mondays (Somvar) — Shiva's day; (2) Maha Shivaratri night (Phalgun Krishna Chaturdashi) — the great night of Shiva, when the four-prahara worship is performed; (3) all of Shravan month (July-August), especially Shravan Mondays; (4) Pradosha Vrata twilight (90 minutes around sunset on Trayodashi); (5) Solah Somvar Vrat (16 Mondays); (6) Kartik Purnima; (7) at Jyotirlinga shrines such as Kashi Vishwanath, Mahakaleshwar, Somnath, Rameshwaram, and Kedarnath. The 8-minute recitation suits both daily devotional sittings and special-occasion worship. Traditional method: facing north or east, before a Shiva Linga, with bilva leaves and water offerings. (Source: classical Shaivite recitation tradition.)
